overview: This step compares source and target text units to detect potential translation quality issues, generating an HTML report or a CheckMate session. It can operate on filter events (batch mode with automatic report generation) or raw documents (interactive mode via CheckMate). Checks include whitespace, inline codes, patterns (regex-based), character sets, length constraints, terminology, forbidden terms, LanguageTool grammar, and more. Non-translatable entries are skipped.

includeTextContainers.help:
Controls whether text containers (structural text units that hold sub-flows) are included in the quality checks.

By default only the leaf-level text units are checked. Enable this if your format uses container-level text that also needs QA.

useGenericCodes.help:
Replaces actual inline code content with generic numbered placeholders (e.g. `<1>`, `</1>`) before comparing source and target codes.

This is useful when target codes legitimately differ from source codes in their internal content but must still match in count and order.

outputPath.help: File path where the QA report is written after processing completes.
outputPath.values: Supports `${rootDir}` and other Okapi pipeline variables.
examples: `${rootDir}/qa-report.html` — default, writes to the pipeline root

outputType.help: Selects the format of the generated QA report.

autoOpen.help:
Automatically opens the generated report in the system default application (browser for HTML, editor for text) when processing completes.

Disable this for automated/CI pipelines where no desktop environment is available.

leadingWS.help: Flags an issue when the target text has different leading whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) compared to the source.

trailingWS.help: Flags an issue when the target text has different trailing whitespace compared to the source.

emptyTarget.help: Flags an issue when the target text is empty while the source is not. Catches untranslated segments.

emptySource.help: Flags an issue when the source text is empty. Empty source entries may indicate extraction problems.

targetSameAsSource.help:
Flags an issue when the target text is identical to the source text. Helps catch untranslated segments.

Several related options refine this check to reduce false positives for segments that legitimately remain unchanged.

targetSameAsSourceForSameLanguage.help:
Disables the "target same as source" check when the source and target languages are the same (e.g. en-US → en-GB).

Useful for locale variants where most text is expected to remain identical.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when targetSameAsSource is enabled

targetSameAsSourceWithCodes.help: Allows target to match source without flagging when the text unit contains inline codes. Segments that are mostly codes (e.g. ``) often need no translation.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when targetSameAsSource is enabled

targetSameAsSourceWithNumbers.help: Allows target to match source without flagging when the text unit is purely numeric (e.g. `100`, `3.14`). Numbers typically do not change between languages.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when targetSameAsSource is enabled

codeDifference.help: Checks that inline codes present in the source also appear in the target, and vice versa. Detects missing, extra, or mismatched codes that could break formatting or produce invalid output.

guessOpenClose.help: Attempts to determine whether isolated codes are opening or closing tags based on their content. Improves code-checking accuracy when the filter does not explicitly mark code directionality.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when codeDifference is enabled

strictCodeOrder.help:
Requires inline codes to appear in the **same order** in source and target. By default only the presence of codes is checked, not their sequence.

Enable for formats where code order affects rendering (e.g. nested HTML tags).

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when codeDifference is enabled

extraCodesAllowed.help:
Number of extra codes allowed in the target that are not in the source, before an issue is flagged.

Useful when translators legitimately add formatting (e.g. bold) not present in the source.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when codeDifference is enabled

missingCodesAllowed.help:
Number of source codes that may be absent from the target before an issue is flagged.

Useful for formats where some codes are optional in the target.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when codeDifference is enabled

checkXliffSchema.help: Validates XLIFF documents against the XLIFF schema. Only applies when the input is an XLIFF file.

xliffSchemaType.help: Selects which XLIFF schema to validate against.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when checkXliffSchema is enabled

checkPatterns.help:
Enables regex-based pattern verification. When enabled, patterns defined in the pattern slots (0–7) are matched against source and/or target text to verify consistency.

Use patterns to verify that numbers, URLs, placeholders, email addresses, and other non-translatable tokens are preserved correctly in the translation.

usePattern0.help: Enables pattern slot 0. Each slot defines a source regex, a target regex, and comparison rules.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when checkPatterns is enabled

fromSourcePattern0.help: When enabled, the source pattern is used to find matches in the source text and the target pattern finds matches in the target — then the **counts** are compared. When disabled, only the target pattern is checked independently.

singlePattern0.help: When enabled, treats the source and target as a single combined text for pattern matching rather than comparing counts between them separately.

severityPattern0.help: Sets the severity level for issues found by this pattern.

sourcePattern0.help: Java regular expression matched against the source text. Use `<same>` to re-use the target pattern.
sourcePattern0.values: Java regex syntax. Special value `<same>` copies the other pattern.

targetPattern0.help: Java regular expression matched against the target text. Use `<same>` to re-use the source pattern.
targetPattern0.values: Java regex syntax. Special value `<same>` copies the other pattern.

descPattern0.help: Human-readable description shown in the report when this pattern triggers an issue.

fromSourcePattern1.help: When enabled, compares match counts between source and target. When disabled, only checks the target.

singlePattern1.help: Treats source and target as a single combined text for matching.

sourcePattern1.help: Java regex for source text. Use `<same>` to copy the target pattern.

targetPattern1.help: Java regex for target text. Use `<same>` to copy the source pattern.

descPattern1.help: Description shown in the report for this pattern.

fromSourcePattern2.help: When enabled, compares match counts between source and target.

singlePattern2.help: Treats source and target as a single combined text for matching.

sourcePattern2.help: Java regex for source text. Use `<same>` to copy the target pattern.

targetPattern2.help: Java regex for target text. Use `<same>` to copy the source pattern.

descPattern2.help: Description shown in the report for this pattern.

fromSourcePattern3.help: When enabled, compares match counts between source and target.

singlePattern3.help: Treats source and target as a single combined text for matching.

sourcePattern3.help: Java regex for source text. Use `<same>` to copy the target pattern.

targetPattern3.help: Java regex for target text. Use `<same>` to copy the source pattern.

descPattern3.help: Description shown in the report for this pattern.

fromSourcePattern4.help: When enabled, compares match counts between source and target.

singlePattern4.help: Treats source and target as a single combined text for matching.

sourcePattern4.help: Java regex for source text. Use `<same>` to copy the target pattern.

targetPattern4.help: Java regex for target text. Use `<same>` to copy the source pattern.

descPattern4.help: Description shown in the report for this pattern.

fromSourcePattern5.help: When enabled, compares match counts between source and target.

singlePattern5.help: Treats source and target as a single combined text for matching.

sourcePattern5.help: Java regex for source text. Use `<same>` to copy the target pattern.

targetPattern5.help: Java regex for target text. Use `<same>` to copy the source pattern.

descPattern5.help: Description shown in the report for this pattern.

fromSourcePattern6.help: When enabled, compares match counts between source and target.

singlePattern6.help: Treats source and target as a single combined text for matching.

sourcePattern6.help: Java regex for source text. Use `<same>` to copy the target pattern.

targetPattern6.help: Java regex for target text. Use `<same>` to copy the source pattern.

descPattern6.help: Description shown in the report for this pattern.

fromSourcePattern7.help: When enabled, compares match counts between source and target.

singlePattern7.help: Treats source and target as a single combined text for matching.

sourcePattern7.help: Java regex for source text. Use `<same>` to copy the target pattern.

targetPattern7.help: Java regex for target text. Use `<same>` to copy the source pattern.

descPattern7.help: Description shown in the report for this pattern.

showOnlyPatternDescription.help: Shows only the pattern description in the report, hiding the actual matched text. Produces a cleaner report when the description is self-explanatory.

patternCount.help: Total number of pattern slots available. The default 8 slots (0–7) cover common checks like parentheses, URLs, email addresses, and printf codes.

checkWithLT.help:
Enables grammar and style checking via [LanguageTool](https://languagetool.org/). Requires a running LanguageTool server.

LanguageTool performs rule-based grammar, spelling, and style checks on the target text.

serverURL.help: URL of the LanguageTool HTTP API server.
serverURL.values: A full URL including port, e.g. `http://localhost:8081/`

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when checkWithLT is enabled

ltBilingualMode.help: Sends both source and target text to LanguageTool for bilingual checking rather than target-only. Enables cross-language consistency rules.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when checkWithLT is enabled

saveSession.help: Saves the QA session to a file so it can be reopened later in CheckMate for interactive review, issue management, and re-checking.

sessionPath.help: File path for the saved CheckMate session.
sessionPath.values: Supports `${rootDir}` and other Okapi pipeline variables. File extension is `.qcs`.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when saveSession is enabled

doubledWord.help: Detects consecutively repeated words in the target text (e.g. "the the"). Common typo in translation.

doubledWordExceptions.help:
Semicolon-separated list of words that are allowed to appear consecutively without triggering the doubled-word check.

Some languages have legitimate doubled words (e.g. French "nous nous", German "hatte hatte").
examples: `sie;vous;nous` — default exceptions for common European language patterns

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when doubledWord is enabled

corruptedCharacters.help: Detects characters that may indicate encoding corruption, such as replacement characters (U+FFFD) or unexpected control characters in the target text.

scope.help: Controls which segments are checked based on their translation state.
scope.values: `0` = All segments, `1` = Only approved/final segments, `2` = Only non-approved segments

checkMaxCharLength.help:
Checks whether the target text exceeds a maximum character length relative to the source. Uses a stepped formula: different percentage thresholds apply depending on source length ranges.

This accounts for the fact that short strings expand proportionally more than long ones during translation.
notes: The three threshold parameters (`maxCharLengthBreak`, `maxCharLengthAbove`, `maxCharLengthBelow`) define a two-tier percentage system with a breakpoint.

maxCharLengthBreak.help: Source character count that separates the "short" and "long" thresholds. Sources at or below this length use `maxCharLengthBelow`; sources above use `maxCharLengthAbove`.

maxCharLengthAbove.help:
Maximum allowed target length as a **percentage** of source length, for source texts **longer** than the breakpoint.

For example, `200` means the target can be up to twice as long as the source.

maxCharLengthBelow.help:
Maximum allowed target length as a **percentage** of source length, for source texts **at or below** the breakpoint.

Short strings typically need a more generous threshold since a single extra word can cause a large percentage increase.

checkMinCharLength.help:
Checks whether the target text is too short relative to the source. Uses the same stepped formula as the maximum length check.

Catches overly abbreviated or truncated translations.
notes: The three threshold parameters (`minCharLengthBreak`, `minCharLengthAbove`, `minCharLengthBelow`) define a two-tier percentage system with a breakpoint.

minCharLengthBreak.help: Source character count that separates the "short" and "long" thresholds for the minimum length check.

minCharLengthAbove.help: Minimum allowed target length as a **percentage** of source length, for source texts **longer** than the breakpoint.

minCharLengthBelow.help: Minimum allowed target length as a **percentage** of source length, for source texts **at or below** the breakpoint.

checkStorageSize.help:
Checks the `maxwidth` attribute (from XLIFF `size-info`) to verify the target text does not exceed the declared storage size limit.

This is format-specific metadata — typically used in software localization where UI elements have fixed pixel or character budgets.

checkAbsoluteMaxCharLength.help: Flags any target text that exceeds an absolute character count, regardless of source length. Use for hard limits like database column widths or UI field sizes.

absoluteMaxCharLength.help: The maximum number of characters allowed in the target text when using the absolute length check.

checkAllowedCharacters.help: Verifies that target text only contains characters representable in a specific character encoding. Catches characters that would be lost or corrupted if the output file uses a restricted encoding.
notes: This check is important when targeting legacy encodings like ISO-8859-1 where many Unicode characters have no mapping.

checkCharacters.help: Enables the character set restriction check that validates against the specified charset.

charset.help: The character encoding to validate against. Any target character not representable in this encoding triggers an issue.

extraCharsAllowed.help: Additional characters to allow beyond those in the specified charset. Use for characters that will be escaped or handled specially in the output format.
extraCharsAllowed.values: A string of individual characters to whitelist.

checkTerms.help: Checks that glossary terms are used correctly in the target. Requires a terminology file (TBX, CSV, or tab-delimited) specifying required translations for source terms.

termsPath.help: Path to the terminology file used for term checking.
termsPath.values: Supports TBX, CSV, or tab-delimited files. Supports `${rootDir}` variable.

stringMode.help: Uses exact string matching instead of word-boundary matching for terminology lookups. Enable when terms may appear as substrings or in agglutinative languages.

betweenCodes.help: Only checks text between inline codes for terminology, ignoring text inside code spans. Reduces false positives from code-like content.

checkBlacklist.help: Checks for forbidden terms (words or phrases that must not appear in the translation). Requires a blacklist file.

allowBlacklistSub.help: Allows substring matching for blacklist terms. When disabled, only whole-word matches trigger an issue.

blacklistSrc.help: Also checks the source text against the blacklist, not just the target.

blacklistPath.help: Path to the blacklist file containing forbidden terms (one per line).
blacklistPath.values: Plain text file, one term per line. Supports `${rootDir}` variable.

showFullPath.help: Shows the full file path in the QA report rather than just the filename. Useful when checking multiple files with the same name in different directories.

typesToIgnore.help: Semicolon-separated list of inline code types to exclude from code-difference checking. Use to suppress false positives from code types that are legitimately different between source and target.
examples: `mrk;x-df-s;` — default, ignores MRK elements and certain XLIFF extension codes

processingNotes: Non-translatable entries are automatically skipped — no checks are performed on them.
The QA report is generated at the **end of the batch**, after all input files have been processed.
With **filter events** input: events pass through to the next step while being checked — the step is transparent to the pipeline.
With **raw documents** input: a CheckMate session is created and opened interactively at batch end, allowing the user to browse issues, disable false positives, and regenerate the report.

examples.title: Pattern checking with `<same>`
examples.description: When the target pattern is set to `<same>`, the step reuses the source pattern to match in both source and target, then compares the match counts. If a URL appears twice in the source but only once in the target, an issue is flagged.
examples.config:
```yaml
checkPatterns: true
usePattern4: true
fromSourcePattern4: true
sourcePattern4: "https?:[\\w/\\.:;+\\-~\\%#\\$?=&,()]+"
targetPattern4: "<same>"
descPattern4: "URLs"
severityPattern4: 1
```

examples.title: Length check with stepped thresholds
examples.description: The maximum length check uses a breakpoint to apply different percentage thresholds based on source length. With the defaults: sources ≤20 chars allow up to 350% expansion (e.g. a 10-char source can have a 35-char target), while sources >20 chars allow up to 200% (e.g. a 50-char source can have a 100-char target).
